1: Psalm 126 explicitly references the return from Babylonian exile, though interpreters debate whether it describes the return as past (vv. 1-3) and prays for its completion (vv. 4-6), or whether vv. 1-3 recall an earlier restoration and vv. 4-6 pray for a further return. "When the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion (shuv YHWH et-shivut Tziyyon)" — the phrase shuv shevut (to restore fortunes/captivity) is a technical term for the reversal of exile conditions (Deut 30:3; Jer 29:14; 30:3, 18; Ezek 16:53; 39:25; Amos 9:14). "We were like people in a dream (keholomim)" — the return from Babylon was so improbable, so contrary to all historical expectation (the Mesopotamian empires did not release conquered peoples), that the community experienced it as a dream from which they feared waking. Cyrus's edict of 538 BCE (Ezra 1:1-4) must have had exactly this quality of unreality for those who had accepted exile as a multigenerational permanent reality.
2: "Then our mouths were full of laughter (sehok) and our tongues rang out with shouts of joy (rinnah); the nations were saying, 'The LORD has done great things for them!'" — laughter and the ringing joyful cry are the involuntary, uncontrollable responses to overwhelming joy — the body's speech when ordinary words are insufficient. The nations' testimony is remarkable: the community did not need to claim the miracle — the surrounding nations named it independently. "The LORD has done great things for them" is what the outsiders saw and said; v3 repeats it as the community's own confession. YHWH's faithfulness to his covenant people becomes visible to the nations as a world-historical testimony to divine character — the same mechanism as the Exodus, where Rahab (Josh 2:10-11) and Jethro (Exod 18:9-11) testified to YHWH's power through what they had witnessed.
3: "The LORD has done great things for us — and we are overjoyed (hayinu semehim)" — the community's echo of the nations' witness: the covenant people claim in their own voice what the nations observed from outside. The joy is stated as an achieved state (hayah): "we have come to be overjoyed" — not a momentary feeling but a settled condition resulting from the recognition of what YHWH has done. The pilgrimage community singing this psalm on the ascent to Jerusalem re-lived the restoration moment liturgically: each pilgrimage recapitulated the return from exile, the journey from scattered foreignness (Ps 120) to covenant community joy at Zion (Ps 134).
4: "Restore our fortunes, O LORD (shuvah YHWH et-shevitenu), like the streams that suddenly fill the desert wadis (ka'afiqim banegev)" — the petition uses the distinctive climate of the Negev as its image. The wadis (seasonal riverbeds, afiqim) of the Negev desert are bone-dry channels during the summer months — then, when the winter rains fall, they fill suddenly and catastrophically with rushing water, transforming from dusty gullies into torrents within hours. The restoration of the exiled community is compared to this sudden, dramatic, landscape-changing inrush of water: not a gradual trickle but a flash-flood abundance that fills what was completely empty in one event. The petition asks for the rest of the return to happen with this quality of sudden completeness rather than the slow, partial dribble of historical return.
5: "Those who plant in tears (bedim'ah) will harvest with shouts of joy (berinnah)" — the agricultural wisdom tradition transformed into eschatological promise. In the ancient Near East, the sowing season coincided with the period of maximum agricultural anxiety: the farmer planted seed during uncertain drought conditions, weeping from the anxiety of not knowing if rain would come, or from the sacrifice of using scarce food-grain as seed. The harvest came in joy when the seed that was planted in weeping produced its fruit. The pattern maps directly onto the exile-then-return narrative: the years of Babylonian captivity were the weeping sowing season; the return and restoration are the harvest-joy. Paul extends this pattern: "whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows generously will also reap generously" (2 Cor 9:6); John 16:20-22 applies it to the disciples' grief at the crucifixion becoming joy at the resurrection.
6: "The one who sets out weeping (halok yelech uvacho), carrying the seed bag (nose meshe-hazaral), will come home with joyful shouts (yavo berinnah), loaded down with the harvest (nose alumotav)" — the agricultural image is rendered in its full sequential detail: departure (weeping, carrying seed), return (shouting, loaded with sheaves). The transformation is total: the same person who left weeping returns rejoicing; the same hands that carried the seed bag now carry the harvest bundle. This is not mere consolation (joy will come eventually) but structural promise: the weeping of sowing and the joy of harvest are phases of the same process, inseparable. For the exile community, this meant that the tears of Babylon were not wasted or lost but were precisely the sowing season of which the return was the harvest — a theology of suffering-as-seed that the NT will develop into the theology of the cross as the sowing of the grain of wheat (John 12:24).
